title: US says ceasefire holding. Oil at $100+, ink shortages, and mass rerouting say otherwise -- 2026-05-12
narrative: The Trump administration insists a month-long ceasefire with Iran is 'active and holding.' Iranian counteroffers — demanding lifted naval blockade, recognition of Strait of Hormuz sovereignty, and war reparations — have been dismissed as 'unbelievably weak' and 'totally unacceptable.' The public message: the conflict is winding down, Iran is the obstacle, and diplomacy is progressing.
telemetry: OIL PRICE: Brent crude sustained above $100/barrel through May 2026 (JP Morgan, BBC Business, 2026-05-12) — structurally elevated, not trading noise; JP Morgan explicitly links the floor to Strait of Hormuz disruption
US INFLATION: CPI surged to 3.8%, highest since May 2023 (BBC, 2026-05-12) — the US government itself attributed the spike to energy costs from the Iran war, not seasonal factors

INK SHORTAGE: A major snack conglomerate switched to black-and-white packaging in May 2026; BBC reported the cause as 'effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz having severely disrupted global supplies of energy and petrochemicals' — petrochemical ink supply is a known casualty

SHIPPING REROUTING: Commercial vessels have been transiting around the Cape of Good Hope since 2023 to avoid Middle East conflict zones; this rerouting intensified in May 2026 rather than easing, indicating continued interdiction threat in the Gulf
HEATHROW DIP: London's Heathrow airport reported a passenger volume decline in April 2026 attributed by industry analysts to Iran conflict spillover effects on Middle East travel routes

BLOCKADE ADMISSION: Iran's stated demand for 'lifting of the US naval blockade' is only coherent if active interdiction is physically occurring — the US has not denied a blockade exists, only called Iran's framing 'inaccurate'
analysis: The economic telemetry is unambiguous and self-consistent. A ceasefire that genuinely held would produce declining risk premiums, not sustained $100+ oil. The inflation spike is not a market perception problem — it is a measured government statistic explicitly tied to energy costs from the conflict. The packaging ink shortage is a supply chain oddity that is only notable because it is so specific and verifiable: a corporation changed its physical product because inputs became unavailable due to Hormuz disruption. Together with shipping reroutes and the Heathrow dip, these are not correlations or sentiment — they are physical goods and services showing measurable disruption consistent with a naval blockade, not a ceasefire. The administration frames the ceasefire as 'active' while observable commodity flows, prices, and corporate supply chain decisions all tell a different story.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT] The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed. The ceasefire narrative is inconsistent with the economic record — either the blockade is not covered by ceasefire terms, or the ceasefire is diplomatic theater while kinetic restrictions continue.
